[¥1](/wiki/1_yen_coin "1 yen coin"), [¥5](/wiki/5_yen_coin "5 yen coin"), [¥10](/wiki/10_yen_coin "10 yen coin"), [¥50](/wiki/50_yen_coin "50 yen coin"), [¥100](/wiki/100_yen_coin "100 yen coin"), [¥500](/wiki/500_yen_coin "500 yen coin")

([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 円; [symbol](/wiki/Currency_symbol "Currency symbol"): **[¥](/wiki/Yen_sign "Yen sign")**; [code](/wiki/ISO_4217 "ISO 4217"): **JPY**) is the official currency of Japan. It is the third-most traded currency in the [foreign exchange market](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_market "Foreign exchange market"), after the [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") and the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is also widely used as a third [reserve currency](/wiki/Reserve_currency "Reserve currency") after the US dollar and the euro.

The **yen** ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 円; [symbol](/wiki/Currency_symbol "Currency symbol"): **[¥](/wiki/Yen_sign "Yen sign")**; [code](/wiki/ISO_4217 "ISO 4217"): **JPY**) is the official currency of Japan. It is the third-most traded currency in the [foreign exchange market](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_market "Foreign exchange market"), after the [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") and the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is also widely used as a third [reserve currency](/wiki/Reserve_currency "Reserve currency") after the US dollar and the euro.

The New Currency Act of 1871 introduced Japan's modern currency system, with the yen defined as 1.5 g (0.048 troy ounces) of gold, or 24.26 g (0.780 troy ounces) of silver, and divided decimally into 100 *sen* or 1,000 *rin*. The yen replaced the previous [Tokugawa coinage](/wiki/Tokugawa_coinage "Tokugawa coinage") as well as the various *[hansatsu](/wiki/Hansatsu "Hansatsu")* paper currencies issued by feudal *han* (fiefs). The [Bank of Japan](/wiki/Bank_of_Japan "Bank of Japan") was founded in 1882 and given a monopoly on controlling the money supply.[[3]](#cite_note-BOJ-3)

Following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the yen lost much of its pre-war value as Japan faced a debt crisis and hyperinflation. Under the [Bretton Woods system](/wiki/Bretton_Woods_system "Bretton Woods system"), the yen was pegged to the US dollar alongside other major currencies. After this system was abandoned in 1971 with the [Nixon Shock](/wiki/Nixon_shock "Nixon shock"), the short-lived [Smithsonian Agreement](/wiki/Smithsonian_Agreement "Smithsonian Agreement") temporarily reinstated a fixed exchange rate. However, since the end of that system in February 1973, the yen has been a [floating currency](/wiki/Floating_exchange_rate "Floating exchange rate").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The [Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Ministry_of_Finance_(Japan) "Ministry of Finance (Japan)") and the Bank of Japan have sometimes intervened in the currency market in recent years, to try to slow down exchange rate movements. There were intermittent interventions from 1998 to 2003 and from 2010 to 2011 to curb excessive and speculative appreciation of the yen, and again in 2022 and 2024 to slow down speculative selling of the currency.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The first two interventions were coordinated with respective[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] countries,[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) and the [IMF](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") has repeatedly stated that Japan is "committed to a flexible exchange rate".[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Pronunciation and etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Pronunciation and etymology")]

The name *yen* derives from the Japanese word [圓](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%9C%93 "wikt:圓") (*en*; [[eɴ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese"); lit. 'round'), which borrows its phonetic reading from [Chinese yuan](/wiki/Chinese_yuan "Chinese yuan"), similar to [North Korean won](/wiki/North_Korean_won "North Korean won") and [South Korean won](/wiki/South_Korean_won "South Korean won"). Originally, the Chinese had traded silver in mass called [sycees](/wiki/Sycee "Sycee"), and when [Spanish and Mexican silver coins](/wiki/Spanish_dollar#Asia "Spanish dollar") arrived from the [Philippines](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_the_Philippines "Captaincy General of the Philippines"), the Chinese called them "silver rounds" ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 銀圓; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *yínyuán*) for their circular shapes.[[11]](#cite_note-Mikami2007-11) The coins and the name also appeared in Japan. While the Chinese eventually replaced 圆; 圓 with 元,[[a]](#cite_note-12) the Japanese continued to use the same word, which was given the *[shinjitai](/wiki/Shinjitai "Shinjitai")* form 円 in reforms at the end of World War II.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The spelling and pronunciation "yen" is standard in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), because when Japan was first encountered by Europeans around the 16th century, Japanese /e/ ([え](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E3%81%88 "wikt:え")) and /we/ ([ゑ](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E3%82%91 "wikt:ゑ")) were both pronounced [je]. Accordingly, Portuguese missionaries spelled them as "ye".[[b]](#cite_note-14) By the middle of the 18th century, /e/ and /we/ came to be pronounced [e] as in modern Japanese, although some regions retain the [je] pronunciation. [Walter Henry Medhurst](/wiki/Walter_Henry_Medhurst "Walter Henry Medhurst"), who had neither been to Japan nor met any Japanese people, having consulted mainly a Japanese-Dutch dictionary, spelled some "e"s as "ye" in his *An English and Japanese, and Japanese and English Vocabulary* (1830).[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMedhurst1830296-15) In the early [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era"), the American physician and translator [James Curtis Hepburn](/wiki/James_Curtis_Hepburn "James Curtis Hepburn"), following Medhurst, spelled all "e"s as "ye" in his *A Japanese and English dictionary* (1867); in Japanese, *e* and *i* are slightly [palatalized](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)"), somewhat as in Russian.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHepburn1867-16) That was the first full-scale Japanese-English/English-Japanese dictionary, which had a strong influence on Westerners in Japan and probably prompted the spelling "yen", which appeared in the 2nd edition (1872). Hepburn revised most "ye"s to "e" in the 3rd edition (1886)[[15]](#cite_note-17) to mirror the contemporary pronunciation, except "yen".[[16]](#cite_note-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Japanese currency](/wiki/Japanese_currency "Japanese currency")

### Early history (1868–1876)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history (1868–1876)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/5yen-M3.jpg/250px-5yen-M3.jpg)](/wiki/File:5yen-M3.jpg)

[5 yen coin](/wiki/5_yen_coin "5 yen coin") from 1870 (year 3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/50sen-M3.jpg/250px-50sen-M3.jpg)](/wiki/File:50sen-M3.jpg)

[50 sen coin](/wiki/50_sen_coin "50 sen coin") from 1870 (year 3)

Although the [Edo Shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate") collapsed with the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") and a new government was born, there was no immediate change to the monetary system. During this unstable period, the confusion caused by this form of exchange caused economic turmoil.[[17]](#cite_note-19) The gold (counting money) system of eastern Japan and the silver (weighing money) system of the western Japan were not unified, and the difference in the gold-silver ratio caused a large amount of gold to flow overseas at the end of the Tokugawa shogunate. [Emperor Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji") responded to this by appointing [Ōkuma Shigenobu](/wiki/%C5%8Ckuma_Shigenobu "Ōkuma Shigenobu") as head of Japan's monetary reform program. He worked with [Inoue Kaoru](/wiki/Inoue_Kaoru "Inoue Kaoru"), [Itō Hirobumi](/wiki/It%C5%8D_Hirobumi "Itō Hirobumi"), and [Shibusawa Eiichi](/wiki/Shibusawa_Eiichi "Shibusawa Eiichi") to run the Ministry of Finance, seeking to introduce a modern monetary system into Japan. Ōkuma eventually proposed that coins, which were previously square, be made into circular discs, and that the names of the traditional currencies, [ryō](/wiki/Ry%C5%8D "Ryō") (両), bu (分) and shu (朱), be unified into yen (円), which was accepted by the government.[[18]](#cite_note-20) Other rejected proposals included physical [weight units](/wiki/Japanese_units_of_measurement#Mass "Japanese units of measurement") of "Fun" and "Momme" which never made it past the [pattern stage](/wiki/List_of_Japanese_coinage_patterns#Meiji "List of Japanese coinage patterns").[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-22)[[21]](#cite_note-23)

The first gold yen coins consisted of 2, 5, and 20 yen coins which were struck throughout 1870. Five yen coins were first struck in gold for the Japanese government in 1870 at the [San Francisco Mint](/wiki/San_Francisco_Mint "San Francisco Mint").[[22]](#cite_note-Frossard-24) During this time a new mint was being established at [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka"), but this did not receive the gold bullion needed for coinage until the following year.[[23]](#cite_note-25) Gold bullion was delivered from private Japanese citizens, foreigners, and the Japanese government.[[24]](#cite_note-Hisamitsu-26) Initially the government opted for silver, which would become the standard unit of value, leaving gold coinage as a subsidiary.[[25]](#cite_note-GS-27) While gold coinage could not be produced domestically in 1870, the mint at Osaka could produce silver coins, and these included denominations of 5, 10, 20, and 50 sen.[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[27]](#cite_note-29) None of these coins dated "1870" circulated until the [Meiji government](/wiki/Meiji_(era) "Meiji (era)") officially adopted the "yen" as Japan's modern unit of currency on June 27, 1871.[[28]](#cite_note-30) This Act formally stipulated the adoption of the decimal accounting system of *yen* (1, 圓), *sen* (1⁄100, 錢), and *rin* (1⁄1000, 厘). The new currency was gradually introduced beginning from July of that year.

Japanese yen-denominated paper currency was also conceived with the coins in 1870, as [Meiji Tsuho](/wiki/Meiji_Tsuho "Meiji Tsuho") notes by Italian engraver [Edoardo Chiossone](/wiki/Edoardo_Chiossone "Edoardo Chiossone").[[29]](#cite_note-Chiossone-31) These were released as [fiat currency](/wiki/Fiat_currency "Fiat currency") in denominations of 1, 2, 5, 10, 50, and 100 yen along with subsidiary notes of 10, 20, and 50 sen in 1872. Almost concurrently, the government established a series of national banks modeled after the system in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), which issued national bank notes.

### Satsuma Rebellion and aftermath (1877–1887)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Satsuma Rebellion and aftermath (1877–1887)")]

Massive inflation from the [Satsuma Rebellion](/wiki/Satsuma_Rebellion "Satsuma Rebellion") in 1877 caused a glut of non-redeemable fiat currency notes. The issuance of national fiat banknotes was ultimately suspended in 1880 by then prime minister [Matsukata Masayoshi](/wiki/Matsukata_Masayoshi "Matsukata Masayoshi").[[30]](#cite_note-SCP20-32)[[31]](#cite_note-mono-33) New policies were put into place which included the establishment of a [centralized banking](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") system.[[32]](#cite_note-BOJ_History-34)[[31]](#cite_note-mono-33)

The [Bank of Japan](/wiki/Bank_of_Japan "Bank of Japan") began operations on October 10, 1882, with the authority to print banknotes that could be exchanged for the old Government and National Bank Notes.[[33]](#cite_note-SCP29-35)[[34]](#cite_note-BOJ1885-36) By May 1883, another act provided the redemption and retirement of national bank notes.[[35]](#cite_note-NB1883AND1896-37)[[36]](#cite_note-Americana1-38) The National Bank Act was amended again in March 1896, providing for the dissolution of the national banks on the expiration of their charters.[[35]](#cite_note-NB1883AND1896-37) This amendment also prohibited national bank notes from circulating after December 31, 1899.[[37]](#cite_note-expired-39) In that year, Japan adopted a [gold exchange standard](/wiki/Gold_exchange_standard "Gold exchange standard"), defining the yen as 0.75 g fine gold or US$0.4985.[[38]](#cite_note-40)

This exchange rate remained in place until Japan left the gold standard in December 1931, after which the yen fell to $0.30 by July 1932 and to $0.20 by 1933.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

It remained steady at around $0.30 until the start of the Pacific War on December 7, 1941, at which time it fell to $0.23.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

The sen and the rin were eventually taken out of circulation at the end of 1953.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

### Fixed rate of the yen to the U.S. dollar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Fixed rate of the yen to the U.S. dollar")]

No true exchange rate existed for the yen between December 7, 1941, and April 25, 1949; wartime inflation reduced the yen to a fraction of its prewar value.

After a period of instability, on April 25, 1949, the U.S. occupation government [fixed](/wiki/Fixed_exchange_rate "Fixed exchange rate") the value of the yen at ¥360 per USD through a plan, which was part of the [Bretton Woods system](/wiki/Bretton_Woods_system "Bretton Woods system"), to stabilize prices in the Japanese economy.[[42]](#cite_note-44)

That exchange rate was maintained until 1971, when the United States abandoned the gold standard, ending a key element of the Bretton Woods system, and setting in motion changes that eventually led to [floating exchange rates](/wiki/Floating_exchange_rate "Floating exchange rate") in 1973.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

### Yen and major currencies float

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Yen and major currencies float")]

By 1971, the yen had become undervalued. Japanese exports were costing too little in international markets, and imports from abroad were costing the Japanese too much. This undervaluation was reflected in the [current account balance](/wiki/Current_account_(balance_of_payments) "Current account (balance of payments)"), which had risen from the [deficits](/wiki/Trade_deficit "Trade deficit") of the early 1960s, to a then-large [surplus](/wiki/Trade_surplus "Trade surplus") of US$5.8 billion in 1971. The belief that the yen, and several other major currencies, were undervalued motivated the United States' actions in 1971.

Following the United States' measures to devalue the dollar in the summer of 1971, the Japanese government agreed to a new, fixed exchange rate as part of the [Smithsonian Agreement](/wiki/Smithsonian_Agreement "Smithsonian Agreement"), signed at the end of the year. This agreement set the exchange rate at ¥308 per US$. However, the new fixed rates of the Smithsonian Agreement were difficult to maintain in the face of supply and demand pressures in the foreign-exchange market. In early 1973, the rates were abandoned, and the major nations of the world allowed their currencies to [float](/wiki/Floating_currency "Floating currency").

### Yen adoption in Okinawa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Yen adoption in Okinawa")]

After World War II the [United States-administered Okinawa](/wiki/United_States_Civil_Administration_of_the_Ryukyu_Islands "United States Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands") issued a higher-valued currency called the [B yen](/wiki/B_yen "B yen") from 1946 to 1958, which was then replaced by the U.S. dollar at the rate of $1 = 120 B yen. Upon the [reversion of Okinawa](/wiki/1971_Okinawa_Reversion_Agreement "1971 Okinawa Reversion Agreement") to Japan in 1972 the Japanese yen then replaced the dollar. In light of the dollar's reduction in value from ¥360 to ¥308 just before the reversion, an unannounced "currency confirmation" took place on October 9, 1971, wherein residents disclosed their dollar holdings in cash and bank accounts; US$60 million were entitled to conversion in 1972 at the higher rate of ¥360.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

### Japanese government intervention in the currency market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Japanese government intervention in the currency market")]

In the 1970s, the Japanese government and business people were concerned that a rise in the value of the yen would hurt export growth by making Japanese products less competitive and would damage the industrial base. The government, therefore, continued to intervene heavily in foreign-exchange markets (buying or selling dollars), even after the 1973 decision to allow the yen to float.[[45]](#cite_note-:0-47)

Despite intervention, market pressures caused the yen to continue climbing in value, peaking temporarily at an average of ¥271 per US$ in 1973, before the impact of the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") was felt (this was retroactively called [endaka](/wiki/Endaka "Endaka"), although the term was only coined in 1985). The increased costs of imported [oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") caused the yen to depreciate to a range of ¥290 per US$ to ¥300 per US$ between 1974 and 1976. The re-emergence of trade surpluses drove the yen back up to ¥211 in 1978. This currency strengthening was again reversed by the [second oil shock in 1979](/wiki/1979_energy_crisis "1979 energy crisis"), with the yen dropping to ¥227 per US$ by 1980.[[45]](#cite_note-:0-47)

### Yen in the early 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Yen in the early 1980s")]

During the first half of the 1980s, the yen failed to rise in value, though current account surpluses returned and grew quickly. From ¥221 per US$ in 1981, the average value of the yen actually dropped to ¥239 per US$ in 1985. The rise in the current account surplus generated stronger demand for yen in foreign-exchange markets, but this trade-related demand for yen was offset by other factors. A wide differential in interest rates, with United States interest rates much higher than those in Japan, and the continuing moves to [deregulate](/wiki/Deregulation "Deregulation") the international flow of [capital](/wiki/Financial_capital "Financial capital"), led to a large net outflow of capital from Japan. This capital flow increased the supply of yen in foreign-exchange markets, as Japanese investors changed their yen for other currencies (mainly dollars) to invest overseas. This kept the yen weak relative to the dollar and fostered the rapid rise in the Japanese trade surplus that took place in the 1980s.

### Effect of the Plaza Accord

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Effect of the Plaza Accord")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/JPY_Nominal_Effective_Exchange_Rates_%281970-%29.svg/250px-JPY_Nominal_Effective_Exchange_Rates_%281970-%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:JPY_Nominal_Effective_Exchange_Rates_(1970-).svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/JPY_Real_Effective_Exchange_Rates_%281970-%29.svg/250px-JPY_Real_Effective_Exchange_Rates_%281970-%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:JPY_Real_Effective_Exchange_Rates_(1970-).svg)

JPY nominal and real [effective exchange rates](/wiki/Effective_exchange_rate "Effective exchange rate") (2005 = 100)

In 1985, a dramatic change began. Finance officials from major nations signed an agreement (the [Plaza Accord](/wiki/Plaza_Accord "Plaza Accord")) affirming that the dollar was overvalued (and, therefore, the yen undervalued). This agreement, and shifting supply and demand pressures in the markets, led to a rapid rise in the value of the yen. From its average of ¥239 per US$ in 1985, the yen rose to a peak of ¥128 in 1988, virtually doubling its value relative to the dollar. After declining somewhat in 1989 and 1990, it reached a new high of ¥123 to US$ in December 1992. In April 1995, the yen hit a peak of under 80 yen/US$, temporarily making Japan's economy nearly the size of that of the US.[[46]](#cite_note-48)

### Post-bubble years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Post-bubble years")]

The yen declined during the [Japanese asset price bubble](/wiki/Japanese_asset_price_bubble "Japanese asset price bubble") and continued to do so afterwards, reaching a low of ¥134 to US$ in February 2002. The Bank of Japan's policy of zero interest rates has discouraged yen investments, with the [carry trade](/wiki/Carry_trade "Carry trade") of investors borrowing yen and investing in better-paying currencies (thus further pushing down the yen) estimated to be as large as $1 trillion.[[47]](#cite_note-49) In February 2007, *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* estimated that the yen was 15% undervalued against the dollar, and as much as 40% undervalued against the euro.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

### After the global economic crisis of 2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: After the global economic crisis of 2008")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Currency_gnp_weighted_comparison_1999_2011.svg/330px-Currency_gnp_weighted_comparison_1999_2011.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Currency_gnp_weighted_comparison_1999_2011.svg)

Comparison of the GNP-weighted nominal exchange rates: CHF and JPY versus CNY, EUR, USD, and GBP

However, this trend of depreciation reversed after the [global economic crisis of 2008](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"). Other major currencies, except the [Swiss franc](/wiki/Swiss_franc "Swiss franc"), have been declining relative to the yen.

On April 4, 2013, the Bank of Japan announced that they would expand their asset purchase program by $1.4 trillion in two years. The Bank of Japan hopes to bring Japan from deflation to inflation, aiming for 2% inflation. The amount of purchases is so large that it is expected to double the money supply, but this move has sparked concerns that the authorities in Japan are deliberately devaluing the yen to boost exports.[[49]](#cite_note-51) However, the commercial sector in Japan worried that the devaluation would trigger an increase in import prices, especially for energy and raw materials.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

### A period of rapid global inflation from 2022 onwards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: A period of rapid global inflation from 2022 onwards")]

Further information: [Carry trade](/wiki/Carry_trade "Carry trade")

Since 2022, the yen has depreciated significantly against its peers, due to a variety of factors. Firstly, Japan's prolonged low-interest-rate policy (to tackle domestic [deflation](/wiki/Deflation "Deflation")) has created a [yield](/wiki/Yield_(finance) "Yield (finance)") differential with other countries—notably the US—that have high interest rates (to tackle domestic [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation")), prompting investors to seek higher returns in foreign currencies. This interest rate differential directly affects the price of the yen and serves as one of the drivers behind its depreciation. Widely held expectations of yen depreciation can become self-fulfilling prophecies, affecting the currency's exchange rate.[[51]](#cite_note-Economist_230430-53) To counter this, the Bank of Japan conducted currency interventions of more than ¥9 trillion selling the dollar and buying the yen in the September–October 2022 and April–May 2024 periods respectively.[[52]](#cite_note-54)[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56)

### Redenomination proposals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Redenomination proposals")]

Numerous proposals have been made since the 1990s to [redenominate](/wiki/Redenomination "Redenomination") the yen by introducing a new unit or new yen, equal to 100 yen, and nearly worth one U.S. dollar. This has not happened to date, since the yen remains trusted globally despite its low unit value, and due to the large costs of reissuing new currency and updating currency-reading hardware. The negative impact of postponing upgrades to various computer software until redenomination occurs, in particular, was also cited.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

The obverse side of all coins shows the coin's value in [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") characters as well as the [country name](/wiki/Names_of_Japan "Names of Japan") (through 1945, *Dai Nippon* (大日本; "Great Japan"); after 1945, *Nippon-koku* (日本国; "State of Japan") (except for the 5-yen coin with the country name on the reverse). The reverse side of all coins shows the year of mintage, which is not shown in [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") years, but instead in the [regnal year](/wiki/Regnal_year "Regnal year") of the [reigning emperor](/wiki/Japanese_era_name "Japanese era name"), with the first year of an era called *gannen* (元年). Imperial portraits have never appeared on Japanese coins, as the image of the emperor remains sacred.[[60]](#cite_note-65) The regnal year does not necessarily align with the calendar year. The first coins of the [Reiwa era](/wiki/Reiwa_era "Reiwa era") were minted in July 2019.[[61]](#cite_note-66)

Examples

* Coins minted in 1900 bear the year [明治 (Meiji)](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era") 33, the 33rd year of [Emperor Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji")'s reign
* Coins minted in 1920 bear the year [大正 (Taishō)](/wiki/Taish%C5%8D_era "Taishō era") 9, the 9th year of [Emperor Taishō](/wiki/Emperor_Taish%C5%8D "Emperor Taishō")'s reign
* Coins minted in 1980 bear the year [昭和 (Shōwa)](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dwa_era "Shōwa era") 55, the 55th year of [Emperor Shōwa](/wiki/Hirohito "Hirohito")'s reign
* Coins minted in 2000 bear the year [平成 (Heisei)](/wiki/Heisei_era "Heisei era") 12, the 12th year of Emperor [Akihito](/wiki/Akihito "Akihito")'s reign
* Coins minted in 2020 bear the year [令和 (Reiwa)](/wiki/Reiwa_era "Reiwa era") 2, the 2nd year of Emperor [Naruhito](/wiki/Naruhito "Naruhito")'s reign

In 1897, the silver 1 yen coin was demonetized and the sizes of the gold coins were reduced by 50%, with 5, 10 and 20 yen coins issued.

After the war, brass 50 sen, 1 and 5 yen were introduced between 1946 and 1948. The existing-type holed brass 5 yen was introduced in 1949, the bronze 10 yen in 1951, and the aluminum 1 yen in 1955.

In 1955 the first unholed, nickel 50 yen was introduced. In 1957, silver 100 yen pieces were introduced, followed by the holed 50 yen coin in 1959. These were replaced in 1967 by the existing cupro-nickel 100 yen along with a smaller 50 yen.[[62]](#cite_note-67)

In 1982, the first cupronickel 500 yen coin was introduced. Alongside the 5 [Swiss franc coin](/wiki/Coins_of_the_Swiss_franc "Coins of the Swiss franc"), the 500 yen coin is one of the highest-valued coin to be used regularly in the world, with a value of US$3.19 as of January 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit).[[63]](#cite_note-68)[[64]](#cite_note-69) Because of its high [face value](/wiki/Face_value "Face value"), the 500 yen coin has been a favorite target for counterfeiters, resulting in the issuance in 2000 of the second nickel-brass 500 yen coin with added security features. Continued counterfeiting of the latter resulted in the issuance in 2021 of the third [bi-metallic](/wiki/Bi-metallic_coin "Bi-metallic coin") 500 yen coin with more improvements in security features.

Due to the great differences in style, size, weight and the pattern present on the edge of the coin they are easy for people with visual impairments to tell apart from one another.

|  | Unholed | Holed |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Smooth edge | ¥1 (light) ¥10 (medium) | ¥5 |
| [Reeded](/wiki/Reeding "Reeding") edge | ¥100 (medium) ¥500 (heavy) | ¥50 |

Commemorative coins have been minted on various occasions in base metal, silver and gold.[[65]](#cite_note-70) The first of these were silver ¥100 and ¥1,000 [Summer Olympic coins](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_coins "Summer Olympic coins") issued for the [1964 games](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics"). The largest issuance by denomination and total face value were 10 million gold coins of [¥100,000 denomination](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%8D%81%E4%B8%87%E5%86%86%E7%A1%AC%E8%B2%A8 "ja:十万円硬貨") for the 60th anniversary of reign of the [Shōwa Emperor](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dwa_(1926%E2%80%931989) "Shōwa (1926–1989)") in 1986, totalling ¥1 trillion and utilizing 200,000 kg fine gold. ¥500 commemorative coins have been regularly issued since 1985. In 2008 commemorative ¥500 and ¥1,000 coins were issued featuring Japan's 47 prefectures. Even though all commemorative coins can be spent like ordinary (non-commemorative) coins, they do not normally circulate, and ¥100,000 coins are treated with caution due to the discovery of counterfeits.[[66]](#cite_note-71)

The 1 yen coin is made out of 100% aluminum and can float on water if placed correctly.

### Formerly circulated coinage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Formerly circulated coinage")]

#### Sen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sen")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/50sen-M3.jpg/250px-50sen-M3.jpg)](/wiki/File:50sen-M3.jpg)

50 sen coin from 1870 (year 3 of Meiji)

Main articles: [Half sen coin](/wiki/Half_sen_coin "Half sen coin"), [1 sen coin](/wiki/1_sen_coin "1 sen coin"), [2 sen coin](/wiki/2_sen_coin "2 sen coin"), [5 sen coin](/wiki/5_sen_coin "5 sen coin"), [10 sen coin](/wiki/10_sen_coin "10 sen coin"), [20 sen coin](/wiki/20_sen_coin "20 sen coin"), and [50 sen coin](/wiki/50_sen_coin "50 sen coin")

Subsidiary coins of "sen" (one hundredth of a yen) were initially introduced in 1870 with a silver alloy in denominations of 5, 10, 20 and 50 sen. Copper sen coins in denominations of half, 1, and 2 came three years later, as Japan acquired the technology needed to mint them. The removal of silver from sen coinage began in 1889, when [Cupronickel](/wiki/Cupronickel "Cupronickel") 5 sen coins were introduced. By 1920, this included cupro-nickel 10 sen and reduced-size silver 50 sen coins. Production of the latter ceased in 1938, after which a variety of base metals were used to produce 1, 5 and 10 sen coins during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). While clay 5 and 10 sen coins were produced in 1945, they were not issued for circulation. As with the Rin, coins in denominations of less than 1 yen became invalid at the end of 1953 and were demonetized due to inflation.

#### Rin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Rin")]

Main articles: [1 rin coin](/wiki/1_rin_coin "1 rin coin") and [5 rin coin](/wiki/5_rin_coin "5 rin coin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/1rin-M6.jpg/250px-1rin-M6.jpg)](/wiki/File:1rin-M6.jpg)

One rin coin from 1873 (year 6 of Meiji)

Bronze coins worth one-one thousandth of a yen called "rin" were first introduced in 1873. One rin coins were very small, measuring 15.75 mm in diameter and 0.3 mm in thickness, and co-circulated with [mon](/wiki/Japanese_mon_(currency) "Japanese mon (currency)") coins of the old currency system. Their small size was eventually their undoing, and the rin was abandoned in 1884 due to unpopularity.[[67]](#cite_note-72)[[f]](#cite_note-73) Five rin coins worth one-two hundredth of a yen also used a bronze alloy. These were successor coins to the equally valued [half sen](/wiki/Half_sen_coin "Half sen coin") coin which had been previously minted until 1888. The decision to bring back an equally valued coin was in response to rising inflation caused by [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") which led to an overall shortage of subsidiary coins. The mintage period for five rin coins was brief as they were discontinued after only four years of production due to their sharp decline in monetary value. The overall demand for subsidiary coinage ended as Japan slipped into a post-war recession. Coins worth 1 and 5 rin were eventually officially taken out of circulation at the end of 1953 and demonetized.

The issuance of yen banknotes began in 1872, two years after the currency was introduced. Denominations have ranged from 1 yen to 10,000 yen; since 1984, the lowest-valued banknote is the 1,000 yen note. Before and during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), various bodies issued banknotes in yen, such as the [Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Ministry_of_Finance_(Japan) "Ministry of Finance (Japan)") and the Imperial Japanese National Bank. The [Allied forces](/wiki/Supreme_Commander_for_the_Allied_Powers "Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers") also issued some notes shortly after the war. Since then, the [Bank of Japan](/wiki/Bank_of_Japan "Bank of Japan") has been the exclusive note issuing authority. The bank has issued five series after World War II.

Japan is generally considered a cash-based society, with 38% of payments in Japan made by [cash](/wiki/Cash "Cash") in 2014.[[68]](#cite_note-74) Possible explanations are that cash payments protect one's privacy, merchants do not have to wait for payment, and it does not carry any negative connotation like credit.

At present, portraits of people from the [Meiji period](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era") and later are printed on Japanese banknotes. The reason for this is that from the viewpoint of preventing forgery, it is desirable to use a precise photograph as an original rather than a painting for a portrait.[[69]](#cite_note-75)[[70]](#cite_note-76)

Series E banknotes were introduced in 2004 in ¥1000, ¥5000, and ¥10,000 denominations.

Series F banknotes were introduced on 3 July 2024. They were announced on 9 April 2019 by Finance Minister [Tarō Asō](/wiki/Tar%C5%8D_As%C5%8D "Tarō Asō").[[71]](#cite_note-77) The ¥1000 bill features [Kitasato Shibasaburō](/wiki/Kitasato_Shibasabur%C5%8D "Kitasato Shibasaburō") and *[The Great Wave off Kanagawa](/wiki/The_Great_Wave_off_Kanagawa "The Great Wave off Kanagawa")*, the ¥5000 bill features [Tsuda Umeko](/wiki/Tsuda_Umeko "Tsuda Umeko") and [Wisteria](/wiki/Wisteria "Wisteria") flowers, and the ¥10,000 bill features [Shibusawa Eiichi](/wiki/Shibusawa_Eiichi "Shibusawa Eiichi") and [Tokyo Station](/wiki/Tokyo_Station "Tokyo Station"). The Ministry decided to not redesign the ¥2000 note due to low circulation.

The [EURion constellation](/wiki/EURion_constellation "EURion constellation") pattern is present in the Series D, E and F banknotes.

## Determinants of value

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Determinants of value")]

Beginning in December 1931, Japan gradually shifted from the gold standard system to the managed currency system.[[72]](#cite_note-78)

The [relative value](/wiki/Relative_value_(economics) "Relative value (economics)") of the yen is determined in [foreign exchange markets](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_market "Foreign exchange market") by the [economic forces](/wiki/Economics "Economics") of [supply and demand](/wiki/Supply_and_demand "Supply and demand").[[73]](#cite_note-79) The [supply](/wiki/Supply_(economics) "Supply (economics)") of the yen in the [market](/wiki/Market_(economics) "Market (economics)") is governed by the desire of yen holders to exchange their yen for other currencies to purchase [goods](/wiki/Good_(economics) "Good (economics)"), [services](/wiki/Service_(economics) "Service (economics)"), or [assets](/wiki/Asset "Asset"). The [demand](/wiki/Demand_(economics) "Demand (economics)") for the yen is governed by the desire of foreigners to buy goods and services in Japan and by their interest in investing in Japan (buying yen-denominated real and financial assets).[[74]](#cite_note-80)

Since the 1990s, the Bank of Japan, the country's central bank, has kept interest rates low to spur economic growth. Short-term lending rates have responded to this monetary relaxation and fell from 3.7% to 1.3% between 1993 and 2008.[[75]](#cite_note-81) Low interest rates and low inflation, combined with a ready liquidity, prompted investors to borrow yen in Japan and invest it in other countries (such as the US) with significantly higher [bank rates](/wiki/Bank_rate "Bank rate"), a practice known as the [carry trade](/wiki/Carry_trade "Carry trade"). This has helped to keep the exchange rate of the yen low compared to other currencies, particularly the US dollar.[[51]](#cite_note-Economist_230430-53)

## Historical exchange rate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Historical exchange rate")]

Before the war commenced, the yen traded on an average of 3.6 yen to the dollar. After the war the yen went as low as 600 yen per USD in 1947, as a result of currency overprinting in order to fund the war, and afterwards to fund the reconstruction.

When MacArthur and the US forces entered Japan in 1945, they decreed an official conversion rate of 15 yen to the USD. Within 1945–1946: the rate tanked to 50 yen to the USD because of the ongoing inflation. During the first half of 1946, the rate fluctuated to 66 yen to the USD and eventually plummeting to 600 yen to the dollar by 1947 because of the failure of the economic remedies. Eventually, the peg was officially moved to 270 yen to the dollar in 1948 before being adjusted again from 1949 to 1971 to 360 yen to the dollar.

Beginning in 2022 the yen/dollar rate has become increasingly weaker with each passing month. By July 2024, the price fell to upper ¥161 per $1, marking the lowest exchange rate for the yen in 37.5 years on a nominal effective exchange rate[[81]](#cite_note-88) and the lowest real effective exchange rate since the start of statistics by the [Bank of Japan](/wiki/Bank_of_Japan "Bank of Japan") in 1970.[[82]](#cite_note-89)[[83]](#cite_note-90)[[84]](#cite_note-91)[[85]](#cite_note-92) One of the main reasons behind this fall was the US moving towards higher interest rates, while Japan remained "ultra-low". Other factors include the strength of the US economy and its labor market, while Japan continues to lag behind its peers to bring its economy back to its pre-pandemic size. Japan's trade balance staying in the red is also likely feeding into the weaker yen.[[86]](#cite_note-Bloom_220610-93)[[87]](#cite_note-94)[[88]](#cite_note-95) Interviewed by [The Asahi Shimbun](/wiki/The_Asahi_Shimbun "The Asahi Shimbun") in September 2022, Izuru Kato, chief economist at Totan Research, expressed concern about the sharp fall in its value since 2022.[[89]](#cite_note-Kato_220902-96) and Moneypost reported that the exchange rate instability had made it impossible to exchange the currency for Japanese yen at some exchange offices.[[90]](#cite_note-97) With the rate returning to ¥150 in November 2023, concern deepened as Nikkei sources expected the yen to depreciate further in the future.[[91]](#cite_note-98) *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* (London) expressed concern over the performance of the Bank of Japan, suggested that a systemic risk is posed to the world financial system.[[92]](#cite_note-Economist_231102-99) Other reports of a weakening of the Japanese yen include the [Noto Peninsula earthquake in January 2024](/wiki/2024_Noto_earthquake "2024 Noto earthquake"), where previous earthquakes had seen a temporary appreciation of the yen against the US dollar, but this earthquake reversed the trend and weakened the yen against the US dollar.[[93]](#cite_note-100)
[Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs") expects the Japanese yen to remain very weak in the coming months.[[94]](#cite_note-101)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Japanese_yen&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** 元; *yuán* is not a simplified form of 圆; 圓; *yuán*, but a completely different character. One of the reasons for replacements is said to be that the previous character had too many strokes.[[11]](#cite_note-Mikami2007-11) Both characters have the same pronunciation in [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Mandarin "Standard Mandarin"), but not in Japanese. In 1695, certain Japanese coins were issued whose surface has the character *gen* (元), but this is an abbreviation of the era name *[Genroku](/wiki/Genroku "Genroku")* (元禄).
2. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** It is known that in ancient Japanese there were distinct syllables /e/ /we/ /je/. From middle of the 10th century, /e/ (え) had merged with /je/, and both were pronounced [je], while a *[kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana")* for /je/ had disappeared. Around the 13th century, /we/ (ゑ) and /e/ ceased to be distinguished (in pronunciation, but not in writing system) and both came to be pronounced [je].[[12]](#cite_note-Hashimoto-13)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-61)** Previously minted pure nickel 50 yen coins dated between 1955 and 1966 are still legal tender.[[57]](#cite_note-NIBV-59) These are rarely seen in circulation due to the price of nickel bullion.[[58]](#cite_note-JT-60)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-63)** Previously minted 100 yen silver coins dated between 1957 and 1966 are still legal tender.[[57]](#cite_note-NIBV-59) Silver was dropped from the coinage in 1967, which led to coin [hoarding](/wiki/Hoarding "Hoarding") and silver smuggling outside of the country for melting.[[59]](#cite_note-SI-62) In light of this and their changed design, its unclear if these coins still circulate.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-64)** Two previous versions were not bi-metallic. The first, consisting of 75% copper and 25% nickel, was produced from 1982 to 2000. The second, consisting of 72% copper, 20% zinc, and 8% nickel, was produced beginning in 2000. Both remain in circulation.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-73)** With a diameter of 15.75 mm and a thickness of 0.3 mm, a one-rin coin is smaller than a modern [euro cent](/wiki/1_euro_cent_coin "1 euro cent coin") (16.25 mm and 1.67 mm respectively).
7. **[^](#cite_ref-83)** The total sum is 200% because each currency trade is counted twice: once for the currency being bought and once for the currency being sold. The percentages above represent the proportion of all trades involving a given currency, regardless of which side of the transaction it is on.
